textDisplay: That final 100m from Sydney is just otherworldly. No one else is even close to that back-half speed. The gap she builds through hurdle 8 is already insurmountable.
Love watching this from a coaching standpoint. Her 13-stride pattern off the curve is textbook. The switch at hurdle 7 is so smooth most people miss it watching in real time.
Former D1 400H here - that stride switch looks effortless but it takes years to nail. She makes 14 strides to 13 look like breathing. Incredible athlete.
Budapest 2023 was the best worlds in years. The crowd atmosphere for the finals was electric. This hurdles race was the highlight of the whole championship.
50.65 should not be physically possible. The previous WR was already considered a ceiling. She just keeps breaking physics. The split times at each hurdle are absurd.
I ran 56.2 in college and thought that was decent. Watching this at 50.65 is humbling. Different species of athlete.
Ha I ran 56.8 in college and felt the exact same way watching this. She makes the best in the world look slow. Unreal performance.
Showing this to my varsity girls tomorrow morning. The curve hurdle attack angle breakdown starting at 8:30 is exactly what we've been trying to fix all season.
The lean into curve hurdles is so undercoached at the high school level. Most kids just run straight and wonder why they lose time on hurdles 2-4. This video explains it perfectly.
Just built my first herb spiral following this guide! Used limestone blocks I found locally. The soil layering section was the most helpful - that is what I had been getting wrong.
The microclimate explanation at 6:00 is what makes a herb spiral so clever. You get 3 different growing environments in 4 square feet. Smart design.
Same here! Built mine in Columbus last spring. Rosemary and thyme at the top are thriving. The bottom mint zone is getting a bit aggressive though - wish I had used a root barrier.
Root barrier tip is gold, thank you! I planted spearmint at the base and it is already escaping. Will put a barrier in this weekend before it takes over the whole yard.
This is excellent for my peds clinical rotation. The verbal cueing section is something they do not teach well in school. Real-world language for keeping kids engaged is so important.
My daughter's PT sent us this video. We have been doing these at home between sessions. The animal names for the exercises make it a game - she asks to do her clam shells every morning now.
The progression from bilateral to unilateral balance tasks is well structured. I like how they include the regression options for kids who are not ready for single-leg stance.
Ran my first half at Columbus last year. The Short North neighborhood section around mile 9 is tough but the crowd makes it worth it. Already signed up for 2025.
The Short North section is my favorite part too! I did it in 2022 and 2024. The German Village stretch near mile 4 is beautiful in October. See you at the start line this fall!
I have used the Novice 1 plan for 3 half marathons. Never felt overtrained or injured following it as written. Hal just works.
Started Week 1 today based on this plan. Running a half in April and I have never raced anything longer than a 10K. Hoping the plan gets me there!
Paris DL always has a special atmosphere and the hurdles race this year delivered. The men's race was closer than the women's but both finals were top-level quality.
Does this germination schedule work for Ohio winters? I am in Columbus and trying to start my herbs indoors in late February for the herb spiral I am building in May.
Also in Columbus! I start basil indoors around March 15 and rosemary/thyme around Feb 28. Last frost is typically mid-May here so you need about 8-10 weeks indoors.
Perfect, thank you! That is exactly what I needed. I will start the basil in mid-March then. Really appreciate the Columbus-specific timing.
The Trendelenburg section at 14:30 is really well explained. I share this with parents in my clinic as pre-appointment viewing so they understand what we are evaluating.
My 4-year-old toe walks and the pediatrician said to wait until 5. This video helped me understand what they are watching for. Still anxious but at least I understand the developmental context.
Glasgow indoor worlds was electric. The 200m track always produces weird tactical racing but the hurdles being flat avoids that. Incredible meet.
The 13 vs 14 stride breakdown is something every 400H athlete needs to watch. Most D3 athletes never attempt 13 all the way through. Understanding when to switch is so important.
I ran 13 strides H1 through H5 and 14 the rest in college. The mental prep to maintain 13 into H6 on tired legs is half the battle. This breakdown nails the decision point.
Really helpful perspective from an actual D1 athlete. My coach says H6 is where most races are lost. This confirms it. Did you ever try 13 strides the whole way through?
The proprioceptive input activities starting at 10:00 are ones we have actually tried at home with our son. The heavy work ideas especially. Wish we had found this earlier in the diagnosis process.
My daughter won the 15-16 age group at juniors. Watching this replay for the 5th time. So proud of how far she has come since starting at age 12.
This is the video I needed before I planted mint in my spiral. It has taken over two full zones. At least I know it is not supposed to be planted next to basil now for future rebuilds.
Mint is the bully of the herb world. Root barrier is the only solution once it escapes - or just give mint its own container nearby. I learned this the hard way last summer.
The field depth in this final is insane. The 5th place finisher would have won worlds in 2015. The event has elevated so much in the last decade.
100% agree. When I competed the national qualifying standard was 57.5s. Now sub-55 barely makes the A standard. The training science and coaching has elevated the whole event.
Never thought about placing cilantro in the middle zone. The moisture explanation makes complete sense - I always killed it by planting it at the top next to the thyme. Trying again this year.
Did this build in one Saturday with my teenager. The drip irrigation setup took another 2 hours on Sunday. Best garden investment we have made. Already growing lettuce and tomatoes.
The bird dog regression progressions here are excellent. We use a very similar sequence in our clinic. Nice to have a clean video to share with home exercise programs.

The easy pacing advice is so counterintuitive when you are a beginner. I ran my first two long runs way too fast and paid for it. Finally found a pace that felt almost embarrassingly slow and ran 2:14 at my race.
The elevation chart is really useful. That hill at mile 10 catches people off guard every year. Plan for it and it is fine. Ignore it and you will suffer the last 5K.
That mile 10 hill is real. I walk it every time and make up time after. Save your legs and the last 5K feels manageable. Good advice for all the first-timers.
So glad Colin did this update. My spiral also had issues with the tarragon at month 6 - same thing happened to him. Knowing it came back strong in year 2 gives me hope.
Our PT gave us this video for our son's ankle sprain home program. The alphabet exercise is genius - he thinks it is a game and does it 3x a day without being asked.
The semifinal heats had some real drama this year with the wind readings. The final was absolutely stacked. Everyone in that race would be a World finalist in most years.
The depth of US women's 400H right now is historic. A clean run at nationals might not even make the Olympic team some years. Wild to think about from an athlete perspective.

As a pediatric PT, I love how they emphasize making exercises feel like play. Hip strengthening done as a game = compliance. A kid doing clam shells willingly at home is worth more than perfect clinic form.
That is such a good point about home compliance. We talked about this in my pediatric specialty module - buy-in from the kid is more important than perfect form almost always.
Ohio had strong representation in the 13-14 age group this year. Watching future Olympians here. These kids are so technically sound already.
Coming back to half marathon training after years of track. The Novice 1 plan is humbling for a former sprinter - way more volume than I am used to. But the structure is exactly what I need.
